title: Cooking Oil on Polyester
stainChemistry: Cooking oil = thin fat (vegetable / canola / olive / peanut). Lower viscosity than grease, penetrates polyester faster. Polyester is OLEOPHILIC — once oil is in, fiber holds it tenaciously. Hot water + dryer heat sets oil into polyester permanently AND yellows the fiber over time. Same protocol as grease-polyester (powder preload + Dawn × 2 + cool wash + air-dry) but the timing window is tighter — fresh = quick win, aged = much harder. Polyunsaturated oils (canola, soybean, corn) polymerize fastest under heat.
whyThisWorks: Cornstarch / baking soda preload pulls bulk oil up via capillary action before deep penetration. Dawn (engineered for cooking oil) emulsifies remaining oil. Cool wash + air-dry prevents heat from setting residual oil + yellowing polyester. The honest cap: aged hot-dried oil on polyester is often permanent.
defaultAssumption: Polyester garment / blend / kitchen apron / restaurant wear. Fresh oil = quick win. Aged or hot-dried oil = harder; sometimes permanent.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Cover oil spot with cornstarch / baking soda. 20–30 min absorb. Brush off softly. Repeat with fresh layer if first saturated.
Dawn + cool water, soft toothbrush 10 min. Polyester is oleophilic — extended dwell needed. Cool rinse.

spottingProtocol.agent: Repeat Dawn If Oily Feel Remains
spottingProtocol.instruction: Test with clean fingertip. Oily feel = repeat Dawn. Polyester typically needs 2 passes for cooking oil.
Cool wash. Air-dry. Inspect BEFORE dryer — heat permanently sets oil + yellows polyester.

steps: 2. Generous Dawn + soft toothbrush, 10 min cool.
3. Repeat Dawn pass if oily feel remains.
4. Cool wash + air-dry. Inspect before dryer.
warnings: NEVER hot water — sets oil into polyester permanently
NEVER chlorine bleach on polyester — yellows fiber
NEVER hot dryer until fully clear
Polyester is oleophilic — expect 2 Dawn passes
Powder preload cuts work in half

homeSolutions.instruction: Cooking oil on polyester is a tractable home stain — IF you do powder preload BEFORE Dawn. Polyester is oleophilic (loves oil), so Dawn alone often plateaus. The cornstarch step is the unlock. Two rules: NO hot water (sets oil into polyester permanently) and NO chlorine bleach (yellows polyester).
Cover the oil spot with cornstarch, baking soda, or plain baby powder. 20 to 30 minutes. Soft brush off. Most surface oil comes off this way without water.
Apply a generous amount of Dawn dish soap directly to the spot. Work it in with a soft toothbrush for 10 minutes — polyester needs longer dwell than cotton. Cool rinse.

homeSolutions.agent: Test for Oily Feel — Repeat Dawn If Yes
homeSolutions.instruction: Touch the spot with a clean fingertip. Still feels oily? Repeat the Dawn step. Polyester typically needs 2 Dawn passes for cooking oil.
Cool wash per care label. Air-dry. Inspect BEFORE dryer. Residual oil + dryer heat = permanent on polyester.

steps: 1. Cool water only. NO hot water until fully clear.
3. Generous Dawn + soft toothbrush, 10 min cool.
4. Repeat Dawn if oily feel remains.
5. Cool wash + AIR-DRY. Inspect before dryer.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if hot dryer was used on residual oil (likely permanent), if oil has been there for weeks, or if 3 home rounds plateau.
warnings: NEVER hot water — sets oil + yellows polyester
NEVER hot dryer until fully clear
Polyester loves oil — expect 2 Dawn passes
Powder preload cuts work in half

neverDo: Never hot water on cooking oil + polyester
Never hot dryer until fully clear
fiberSensitivities: Polyester is oleophilic — grabs oil tenaciously
Polyester yellows under heat over time
Polyester typically needs 2 Dawn passes for oil
Polyunsaturated oils polymerize fastest under heat

materialWarnings: Powder preload is the unlock on polyester oil
No hot water / hot dryer — heat sets oil

escalation.when: Hot dryer used on residual oil, weeks-old oil through multiple wash cycles, 3 home rounds plateau.
escalation.whatToTell: "Cooking oil on polyester, age [hours / days / weeks], home-treated with cornstarch preload + Dawn × 2 + cool wash + air-dry. No hot water / no chlorine bleach. [Residual oily feel / yellow shadow]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with solvent spotting

contextQuestions: Type of oil — olive / canola / vegetable / peanut?
How long ago — fresh / dried in / weeks?
Has it been through a hot wash or dryer?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Polyunsaturated oils (canola, soybean) polymerize fastest under heat. Olive oil is slower; extra-virgin slower still. Hot-dried polymerized oil = often permanent on polyester.
